manifest.name: more-agile-testing
manifest.title: More Agile Testing
manifest.subtitle: Learning Journeys for the Whole Team — Gregory & Crispin (2015)
manifest.home-description: Learning Journeys for the Whole Team — Gregory & Crispin (2015)

chapter.title: Evolution of Agile Testing & Organizational Culture

list.item: Testing in agile is an activity, not a phase — it is everyone's responsibility
Agile testing evolved from lone testers on XP teams to whole-team quality ownership
Organizational culture is the #1 enabler (or blocker) of agile testing success
Quality focus leads to sustainable pace; speed-first creates technical debt gridlock

section.paragraph: Agile testing started with individual testers on XP teams where testing wasn't even mentioned. It evolved through several key milestones:

list.item: Driving development with examples — Brian Marick, Joshua Kerievsky pioneered story tests; Ward Cunningham created Fit
BDD — Dan North introduced behavior-driven development, paving the way for modern tools
Agile testing quadrants — Brian Marick's matrix adapted into the quadrants model for planning all test types
Generalizing specialists — T-shaped skills with depth and breadth replace siloed roles
Business analysis integration — BAs bring discovery skills; UX designers bring user research
DevOps convergence — Development, operations, and testing blend for shorter release cycles

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: Testing is an activity that is crucial to product success and is of interest to every member of the product team — from stakeholders through to operations and support.

section.paragraph: Development teams adopt agile values quickly, but the business side often takes longer. For success, both must be in sync.

key-box.heading: Quality vs. Speed
key-box.paragraph: Companies that focus on quality achieve sustainable pace and consistent delivery. Companies that focus on speed first sacrifice quality, accumulate technical debt, and create a death spiral of ever-growing fragility.

section.paragraph: Creativity requires time to think, learn, and experiment. Gojko Adzic notes great results happen when:

list.item: People know why they are doing their work
Organizations focus on delivering outcomes and impacts, not features
Teams decide what to do next based on immediate and direct feedback
Everyone cares

key-box.heading: Capacity Utilization (J-Curve)
key-box.paragraph: Average queue size remains low until ~80% utilization, then rises sharply to infinity. Overutilization kills flow. The purpose of slack (e.g., 20% time) is to keep utilization below the critical threshold — not to fill it with innovation projects. "80 is the new 100" — if you mandate 20% time without understanding the theory, you just shrink capacity.

list.item: Foster learning — Communities of practice, guilds, Lean Coffee sessions, pairing, hack weeks
Short feedback loops — Transparency in decisions builds trust between teams and executives
Educate the organization — Quantify the cost of technical debt; show executives business impact
Managing testers — No one right answer; depends on context. Guilds work for small orgs; test managers as coaches for larger ones
Focus on quality for long-term sustainable pace
Build slack by improving delivery capability and managing scope
Nurture a learning culture with time for experiments
Educate stakeholders on how good practices pay back
Make process visible to build trust
Celebrate successes, big and small
Emphasize competencies over roles — "How can I help?" replaces "It's not my job"
T-shaped skills — broad knowledge for collaboration + deep expertise for contribution
Thinking skills (facilitation, feedback, problem solving) are as critical as technical skills
Learning is itself an agile, iterative process — not a big-bang event

section.paragraph: Testers need broad knowledge (architecture, coding concepts, databases, CI tools) plus deep testing expertise. Team members in other roles need basic testing concepts in their T-bar.

key-box.heading: Square-Shaped Team
key-box.paragraph: The true power of T-shaped individuals emerges when they combine into a square-shaped team — each tester brings unique deep skills with the same breadth, complementing each other to cover all challenges.

section.paragraph: Not interchangeable generalists, but specialists who can appreciate, communicate with, and collaborate across disciplines. Key technique: learn the heuristics (rules of thumb) of other specialties to help with signal detection, even without deep expertise.

list.item: Hire for attitude and curiosity — skills can be trained
Set realistic expectations: day 1, week 1, month 1 milestones
Pairing is the best onboarding method — pair with testers, programmers, BAs, DevOps, business experts
Fresh eyes find bugs that veterans ignore — never discount what a new tester observes

row.cell: Guide specification workshops; ensure each participant feels free to express ideas
5 Whys, fishbone diagrams, impact maps — address root causes, not symptoms
Focus on the work, not the person. Empathy is essential. Listen to learn.
Deep domain knowledge prevents bugs and generates feature ideas
Guide others to solve their own problems; storytelling depersonalizes criticism
Recognize L-Mode (linear) vs R-Mode (intuitive); use the right mode for the situation
Risk-based testing to focus on right tasks; avoid thrashing on repeated tests
Define goal → language → process → time-box → follow → assess → repeat

list.item: Guiding development with examples — ATDD, BDD, SBE turn customer examples into automated tests
Automation & coding — Use same IDE as coders; understand architecture; learn SOLID principles
General technical skills — Monitor processes, read logs, basic SQL, command-line comfort
Test environments — Know what code/data is on each environment; maintain visibility
CI & source control — Understand CI configurations; know which failures block your testing
Quality attributes (Q4) — Security, performance, reliability — often the weakest testing area
Test design techniques — State transitions, decision tables, domain testing
Know your learning style — auditory, visual, kinesthetic; emotions affect intake
Conferences & meetups — Network with practitioners; hallway conversations are the most valuable
Pairing — The fastest way to transfer skills; pair across roles and teams
Communities of practice — Book clubs, journal clubs, lunch-and-learns
Practice — Code retreats, testing dojos, Weekend Testing sessions
Teach to learn — Teaching a skill to someone else is the best way to learn it yourself

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: Learning is an agile process: add a bit more knowledge each iteration, build on it the next. Don't treat it as a big-bang event.

chapter.title: Planning — Don't Forget the Big Picture

list.item: Agile teams plan more than waterfall teams — in smaller chunks, just-in-time, with fast feedback
Use levels of precision: release → feature → story → task
The agile testing quadrants are a taxonomy, not a sequence
The automation pyramid guides where to automate for best ROI

row.cell: UAT, load, performance, integration, cross-team dependencies
Acceptance tests, quality attributes, usability, mind maps of test ideas
High-level acceptance tests, story tests, exploratory charters
TDD — write test first, then code; less planning, more doing

section.paragraph: Automated regression tests don't happen by chance. Plan to update them as functionality changes. Organize by system functionality, not by story — story-based grouping becomes unmaintainable after six months.

list.item: Mind maps — Brainstorm test ideas; track progress with completion markers
Test matrix — Rows = features, columns = test conditions; color-code progress
One-page test plan — Keep it lightweight; mind maps satisfy even document-demanding managers

row.cell: Business-facing tests that critique the product
Technology-facing tests that critique the product

list.item: Numbering does not imply order — Q1 through Q4 is arbitrary
Left side = preventing defects before/during coding; right side = finding defects
"Facing" refers to the language of the tests, not who runs them
Most teams use techniques from all quadrants concurrently
Replaces "guide/critique" with "checking expected results" vs "analyzing unknowns"
Better for continuous delivery and lean startup contexts
Makes exploratory testing and production monitoring more visible
Replaces horizontal axis with "confirm" vs "investigate"
Highlights the difference between verifying known behavior and discovering unknowns
Useful for teams that have matured beyond the original model
FURPS+ — Functionality, Usability, Reliability, Performance, Supportability + constraints
ACC Matrix — Attributes (adjectives), Components (nouns), Capabilities (verbs) for risk-based testing
Automation Pyramid — Push tests to lowest level for fastest feedback and best ROI
Testing is more than verifying code — it's about testing ideas, assumptions, and business value
Start with "why" before "what" — understand the business problem before building
Examples are the heart of guiding development — ATDD, BDD, SBE all start with conversations
Having conversations > capturing conversations > automating conversations

section.paragraph: When we ask why customers want a feature, we're more likely to build the right thing. Customers often tell us what to implement without explaining the problem they're solving. Understanding the purpose often reveals simpler solutions.

row.cell: Identify alternative paths to business goals
What would personas do first? Next?
Explore all aspects of product needs
User, Interface, Action, Data, Control, Environment, Quality

list.item: User — Who uses/benefits from the product?
Interface — How do users connect with the product?
Action — What are the product's capabilities?
Data — What data is received, validated, stored?
Control — What constraints/rules must be enforced?
Environment — Where/how will it be used and deployed?
Quality Attribute — What service levels for operational qualities?

section.paragraph: Blurred roles make jobs more interesting. Testers can learn from:

list.item: Business Analysis — Facilitate structured conversations; remember all product dimensions; start with "why"
UX Design — Usability testing, paper prototypes, A/B testing, focus groups
Documentation — Challenge ideas by articulating concepts; add "Create content" task to stories

key-box.heading: Key Takeaway
key-box.paragraph: When you see a problem, take the initiative to get the right people together. Whoever has the initiative can lead the way — regardless of role.

row.cell: Tests define scope; story is "done" when checks pass
"Should" invites questioning assumptions; "test" presupposes known behavior
Derive scope from goals → key examples → refine → automate → living documentation

list.item: Concrete examples make abstract requirements understandable for all roles
Reduce uncertainty about what to build; prevent defects before coding
DSL format enables automation as a side effect of specification
Programmers use examples for TDD; creates shared language across the team
Too much detail — overwhelms programmers; may signal design problems
Lacking buy-in — stakeholders feel too busy; programmers prefer TDD only
Too many regression tests — evaluate ROI before adding every test to CI suite
Not enough known yet — spike first when requirements are emergent
Start with happy path; add complexity as coding progresses
Propose experiments; show benefits with short, focused meetings
Prune low-value tests from regression suites regularly
Use spike solutions for quick feedback when unknowns are high
Exploratory testing combines test design with test execution — learning about the application while testing
ET and automation are complementary — automation handles checking; ET finds what wasn't anticipated
Don't neglect non-functional testing: concurrency, i18n, security, UX, regression

section.paragraph: Exploratory testers don't enter with predefined expected results. They compare system behavior against expectations based on experience, heuristics, and oracles.

key-box.heading: Charter Template (Hendrickson)
key-box.paragraph: Good charters are prompts: they suggest sources of inspiration without dictating precise actions or outcomes. Better to have multiple concise charters than one broad one.

row.cell: Test as different user types — security-worried user, help desk admin, bad customer
Visit software "landmarks" in different sequences; combine with personas
Take personas through complete workflows to test end-to-end flow
Target architectural risks identified by programmers
Invalid input, time changes, unusual uses, too much, stop halfway, wrong assumptions

list.item: Time-boxed sessions (30–90 min)
Charter → explore uninterrupted → record → debrief
Good for training new testers via debrief feedback
Can compress regression testing to fit within an iteration
Organized around threads of activity, not time boxes
More flexible for rapidly changing priorities
Mind maps track threads, notes, defects, and status
Naturally focuses testing on problem/risk areas as discovered
Group hugs — Whole team explores concurrently; diversity creates different discoveries
Especially valuable for concurrency testing and major releases
Trinity Testing — Programmer + tester + BA for fast multi-perspective feedback

row.cell: Integration testing, high-risk workflows, tours, end game
Complete feature workflow, interaction with other apps, group hugs
Programmer exploration during coding, tester-programmer pairing
Test simultaneous updates; use group hugs + automated regression tests; timing issues are subtle
System is ready to be translated; design encoding, formatting, string externalization into the system
System is translated; use locals for localizations; beware unit conversions and cultural nuances
Automate for fast feedback; organize by functionality; consider testing in production for frequent releases
Bring users into development early; don't wait for end game; "making sure users can do their job"
Two implementations in production; measure which performs better; iterate with fast feedback
Paper prototypes before coding; functional prototypes; contextual inquiry with real users

list.item: Automated test code is as valuable as production code — treat it with the same care
The whole team owns automation — not just testers
Push tests to the lowest level that makes sense for fastest feedback and best ROI
Make test technical debt visible and address the biggest problem first

section.paragraph: Inadequate test automation creates a vicious cycle: no time → skip Q3/Q4 testing → more regression failures → less time for guiding development → wrong features built.

list.item: Write task cards for test maintenance and failure investigation
Track time spent on test maintenance; reflect in story estimates
Use red sticky notes on blocked story cards for bugs
Make CI build status prominent; broken builds = visible debt

key-box.heading: Zero-Tolerance Bug Strategy (Paddy Power)
key-box.paragraph: When a bug is found during exploratory testing, the programmer drops everything and fixes it immediately. No bug tracking tool. No prioritization meetings. The card doesn't progress until the bug is fixed and buried. Result: no known bugs in production.

list.item: No unit tests — Replace with programmer-owned integration tests when code is data-focused
JavaScript-heavy — Expanded top layer for complex front-end logic; hourglass shape
Eye of Providence — Exploratory testing as the capstone ensuring confidence in automated tests
Test Volcano — Without automation at lower levels, lava (debt) builds until eruption buries the team
Expanded pyramid — Add dimensions for tools, test types, and quality attributes on each face
Each test has a single clear purpose
DRY — Extract duplication into macros and modules
Use Arrange-Act-Assert pattern at all levels
Treat test code with same design care as production code
Document your team's test design patterns for consistency

row.cell: Create order qualifying for free shipping
Open page, type in fields, click buttons

section.paragraph: Encapsulate each page as an object with its elements and operations. When the UI changes, update only the Page Object — not every test that uses it. Operations return Page Objects (e.g., login returns dashboard).

list.item: Tests must create their own data before running
Tests must clean up data after execution
Use randomized but realistic data (Ruby Faker, jdefault)
Never rely on shared, long-lived test data — it becomes untrustworthy
Decide how tests should look first, then find tools — not the other way around
Choose frameworks that foster collaboration between testers and programmers
Use bake-offs — two pairs try different approaches; team evaluates results
For large orgs: define classes of acceptable tools for consistency with autonomy
Not every test needs to run in CI — slow suites can run nightly or pre-release
Push automation to lowest level; refactor architecture if needed to enable it

key-box.heading: Collaborative vs Non-Collaborative Tools
key-box.paragraph: Collaborative tools: tests in version control, readable by business, written by programmers + testers together, integrated with CI. Non-collaborative: record-and-playback, proprietary formats, tester-only, separate from code.

list.item: Agile principles apply in any context — enterprise, distributed, mobile, regulated, data, DevOps
Context magnifies challenges but doesn't change fundamentals
Adapt practices to your situation; don't blindly copy others
System test team — Tests the integrated product end-to-end in production-like environments
Extended sprint test team — Takes accepted stories + automated tests; runs on extended compatibility configs
Consistent tooling — Common CI across teams; automated regression catches integration issues on check-in
Version control — Prefer all teams on same code base; reduces integration and testing risks
Managing dependencies — Use "Ready" status on stories; remove dependencies before pulling into iteration
Test architects — Coordinate testing across teams; evolve automation strategy
Started with pilot project (programmers only) → proved value → integrated testers
Shifted from UI-only automation to service-level automation first
Removed requirement to log all defects → encouraged real-time collaboration
Added Q4 testing (longevity, scalability, performance baselining)
Result: Test is now a full partner in the entire software development process

row.cell: Coaching on both sides; prepare ideas via email first; don't use "cultural issues" as excuse
Speak slowly; paraphrase; summarize decisions in writing; avoid slang
Overlap hours for collaboration; async tools for everything else; record meetings
Visualize dependencies; plan cross-team integration points early

list.item: Collaborate through tests — Tests become the shared language across locations
Tools — Video conferencing, shared wikis, mind-mapping tools, Google Docs for real-time collaboration
Testing is critical — high risk for safety and market success
Use Page Objects for mobile UI automation; test on real devices
Embedded systems require hardware-in-the-loop testing; plan for longer feedback cycles

key-box.heading: The "Lack of Documentation" Myth
key-box.paragraph: Agile does not mean no documentation. Automated tests provide living documentation. Traceability can be achieved through test-to-requirement mapping. Agile can meet regulatory demands — the key is making testing activities and their artifacts visible.

list.item: Data is the critical asset — test data quality, not just code quality
Use agile principles: incremental delivery, short feedback loops
Big Data requires specialized testing approaches for volume, velocity, variety
DevOps blends development, operations, and testing for faster delivery
Testers add value through: monitoring production, defining quality gates in deployment pipelines, testing infrastructure as code
Shared responsibility for quality across the entire delivery chain
Visualize testing to communicate importance and drive continuous improvement
Build confidence through a combination of checking and exploring
Create a shared vision for testing that involves the whole team
Communicate importance — Make testing visible to stakeholders through dashboards, mind maps, test matrices
Continuous improvement — Track test maintenance time, failure investigation time, coverage gaps
Test results visibility — CI dashboards, big visible charts, shared documents for exploratory sessions

key-box.heading: Big Visible Charts for Testing
key-box.paragraph: Claire Moss's evolution: personal bug board → severity-based → site-map clustering → integrated into sprint backlog. Each iteration revealed team needs and drove process improvement. The chart itself became a testing tool that opened communication.

row.cell: Shared understanding; prevents defects before coding
Finds what wasn't anticipated; critiques the product
Adapts to changing technology and business needs
Adapts practices to the specific situation
Focus on actual business value, not process compliance

section.paragraph: The whole team — stakeholders, programmers, testers, designers, DevOps — must share ownership of quality. Testing is not a phase or a role; it's an activity woven into every aspect of software development.

list.item: Testing is an activity, not a phase
Quality is the whole team's responsibility
Start with examples and conversations before code
Automate regression checks at the lowest effective level
Explore to find what automated tests can't
Visualize testing status for the whole team
Experiment — try small changes, evaluate, iterate
Adapt practices to your context — there is no one right way

syllabus.footer-text: Based on "More Agile Testing: Learning Journeys for the Whole Team" by Janet Gregory & Lisa Crispin (Addison-Wesley, 2015)
